content.body: Plants cannot move from place to place but they do respond to stimuli by growing towards or away from them. These growth responses are called TROPISMS.

PHOTOTROPISM: Growth response to light.
• Positive phototropism: shoot grows TOWARDS light
• Negative phototropism: root grows AWAY from light

GRAVITROPISM (GEOTROPISM): Growth response to gravity.
• Positive gravitropism: root grows DOWNWARD (towards gravity)
• Negative gravitropism: shoot grows UPWARD (away from gravity)

THIGMOTROPISM: Growth response to touch/contact. Example: pea tendrils wrapping around a support.
HYDROTROPISM: Root growth towards water.
CHEMOTROPISM: Growth in response to chemicals (e.g. pollen tube grows towards ovule).

AUXINS: Plant hormones (mainly IAA — Indole Acetic Acid) that control tropisms.
Auxin causes cell elongation.
In shoots: auxin collects on the SHADED side → that side grows faster → shoot bends TOWARDS light.
In roots: roots are more sensitive to auxin; high concentration INHIBITS growth.

content.title: Phototropism Mechanism
content.body:
1. Light hits one side of shoot tip
2. Auxin moves to the shaded side
3. Shaded side cells elongate more
4. Shoot curves/bends towards the light source
This was shown by Boysen-Jensen's experiment cutting and replacing the shoot tip.

questions.question: Phototropism is a plant's growth response to:
questions.explanation: Photo = light. Phototropism = growth in response to light stimulus.

questions.question: A shoot growing towards light is showing:
questions.explanation: Positive tropism = growing TOWARDS the stimulus. Shoot grows towards light = positive phototropism.

questions.question: Roots show positive gravitropism because they grow:
questions.explanation: Roots need to grow down into soil for water and anchorage. They are positively gravitropic.

questions.question: Auxin (IAA) causes plant cells to:
questions.explanation: Auxin stimulates cell elongation by loosening cell walls and allowing water uptake, making cells longer.

questions.question: In a shoot exposed to light from one side, where does auxin accumulate?
questions.explanation: Auxin moves away from light to the shaded side. This side elongates more, bending the shoot towards light.

questions.question: Pea tendrils wrapping around a trellis is an example of:
questions.explanation: Thigmo = touch. Tendrils respond to contact with a support by coiling around it.

questions.question: Which part of the shoot detects the direction of light?
questions.explanation: The shoot tip contains the cells that detect light. If the tip is removed, the shoot does not respond to light.

questions.question: In roots, high concentration of auxin:
questions.explanation: Roots are more sensitive to auxin. The higher concentration on the lower side inhibits root growth there, while the upper side grows more — root bends downward.

questions.question: Hydrotropism is growth towards:
questions.explanation: Hydro = water. Hydrotropism = growth in response to water gradient. Roots grow towards moisture.

questions.question: A seedling is placed on its side in the dark. After a few days the shoot will grow:
questions.explanation: Gravitropism: shoot is negatively gravitropic — it grows away from gravity (upward), even in the dark.

questions.question: Tropisms are described as positive when growth is:
questions.explanation: Positive tropism = towards stimulus. Negative tropism = away from stimulus.

questions.question: Auxins are produced mainly in the:
questions.explanation: Auxin (IAA) is primarily synthesised in meristematic tissue at shoot tips and transported downward.

questions.question: If the tip of a shoot is covered with a light-proof cap, the shoot:
options: Does not respond to light (grows straight)
questions.answer: Does not respond to light (grows straight)
questions.explanation: The tip detects light. Covered tip = no signal detected = no unequal auxin distribution = straight growth.

questions.question: Nastic movements differ from tropisms because they:
options: Are not directional (direction not determined by stimulus direction)
questions.answer: Are not directional (direction not determined by stimulus direction)
questions.explanation: Nastic responses (e.g. flower opening, Mimosa leaf folding) are not directional — the direction is fixed regardless of stimulus direction.

questions.question: Pollen tube growing towards the ovule through the style is:
questions.explanation: Pollen tube grows in response to chemical signals from the ovule — this is chemotropism.

questions.question: The experiment that proved auxin was responsible for phototropism was done by:
questions.explanation: Boysen-Jensen (1910s) showed that a chemical signal (auxin) from the shoot tip caused bending by using mica and gelatin inserts.

questions.question: Shoot showing negative gravitropism grows:
questions.explanation: Shoots are negatively gravitropic — they grow upward, away from the pull of gravity.

questions.question: In commercial fruit production, synthetic auxins are used to:
options: Set fruit without pollination and prevent premature fruit drop
questions.answer: Set fruit without pollination and prevent premature fruit drop
questions.explanation: Synthetic auxins (like 2,4-D or NAA) are used as weedkillers at high dose, and to improve fruit set/retention at low dose.

questions.question: Root growing towards a water source is:
questions.explanation: Root grows TOWARDS water = positive hydrotropism. Ensures plant accesses water supply.

questions.question: Tropisms are important to plants because they:
options: Allow plants to optimise light capture, water absorption and anchorage without moving
questions.answer: Allow plants to optimise light capture, water absorption and anchorage without moving
questions.explanation: Shoots grow towards light (photosynthesis). Roots grow down (anchorage) and towards water. All without locomotion.
